draft.title: a CRM for freelancers
draft.summary: Drafting your prototype from the swipes…
draft.html:
a CRM for freelancers

Building your first draft…

facade.hypothesis: Freelancers need micro-interactions that feel fast and automatic, reducing conscious form-submission friction
facade.label: Instant save on blur
facade.acceptImplies: User values seamless state persistence and ambient automation in CRM data capture
facade.rejectImplies: User prefers explicit, intentional control moments (save buttons, confirmation states) in workflow

facade.hypothesis: Freelancers prefer a friendly, approachable tone that feels like working with a peer rather than enterprise software
facade.label: Casual and warm
facade.acceptImplies: The CRM should use conversational language, gentle humor, and human-centered design to feel less corporate and more like a trusted colleague
facade.rejectImplies: Users may expect a more polished, professional, business-standard voice—treating the CRM as a serious productivity tool where casual tone feels unprofessional or distracting

facade.hypothesis: Freelancers prefer approachable, non-corporate interfaces with gentle visual weight and rounded forms over sharp, minimalist designs
facade.label: Soft shadows and curves
facade.acceptImplies: Design direction favors organic, friendly aesthetics—rounded buttons, subtle depth, warm tones; avoid stark minimalism
facade.rejectImplies: Design direction skews toward crisp, efficient, minimal—flat shapes, high contrast, cooler tones; approachability is lower priority

evidence.hypothesis: Freelancers need micro-interactions that feel fast and automatic, reducing conscious form-submission friction
evidence.implication: User values seamless state persistence and ambient automation in CRM data capture

facade.hypothesis: A warm, earthy-toned navigation panel with soft edges and organic typography would feel more inviting and human for a freelancer CRM than a cool, minimalist sidebar.
facade.axisTargeted: Warm vs. Cool — does the freelancer interface lean into approachable warmth or clinical efficiency?
facade.label: Warm sidebar
facade.acceptImplies: Users prefer interfaces that feel personally connected and less corporate. Future probes should explore organic, textured, or hand-crafted aesthetics.
facade.rejectImplies: Users want the interface to feel professional, trustworthy, and visually clean. Future probes should explore cool palettes, sharp typography, and minimal design patterns.

facade.hypothesis: Freelancers need quick access to clients, projects, and tasks without leaving context — a persistent sidebar reduces friction
facade.axisTargeted: Navigation and spatial organization (sidebar vs top tabs)
facade.label: Sidebar nav
facade.acceptImplies: User values vertical space efficiency and persistent context switching over a clean, uncluttered top bar
facade.rejectImplies: User prefers prominent, scannable top navigation or might find sidebars claustrophobic for a freelancer's varied workday

facade.hypothesis: Freelancers need dense data overviews more than they need narrative summaries of client relationships
facade.axisTargeted: Information density and scanning speed vs contextual narrative
facade.label: Dashboard tables vs client cards
facade.acceptImplies: CRM should prioritize tabular views with sortable columns (invoices, rates, project history) where data comparison is the primary task
facade.rejectImplies: CRM should favor card-based layouts that surface key client info and relationship narrative in a scannable, hierarchical way

axes.label: Whether they prefer minimal, clean interfaces vs. rich visual detail and color—reveals if they want distraction-free focus or visual richness to feel professional
Whether they favor dashboard-centric overview vs. list/table-based workflows—reveals how they mentally organize client and project information
Whether they want a business-formal tone vs. casual, encouraging tone—reveals if they see the tool as serious infrastructure or supportive partner
Whether they prefer calm, minimal atmosphere vs. energetic, motivating atmosphere—reveals emotional context they work best in
Whether they want all client info visible at once vs. progressive disclosure of details—reveals tolerance for information density and cognitive load
Whether they prefer instant feedback and snappy interactions vs. smooth, deliberate transitions—reveals their pace preference and how they like to feel in control

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether they prefer minimal, clean interfaces vs. rich visual detail and color—reveals if they want distraction-free focus or visual richness to feel professional
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Iris

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether they favor dashboard-centric overview vs. list/table-based workflows—reveals how they mentally organize client and project information
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Prism

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether they want a business-formal tone vs. casual, encouraging tone—reveals if they see the tool as serious infrastructure or supportive partner
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Lumen

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether they prefer calm, minimal atmosphere vs. energetic, motivating atmosphere—reveals emotional context they work best in
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Aura

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether they want all client info visible at once vs. progressive disclosure of details—reveals tolerance for information density and cognitive load
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Facet

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether they prefer instant feedback and snappy interactions vs. smooth, deliberate transitions—reveals their pace preference and how they like to feel in control
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Echo

facade.hypothesis: Freelancers value gesture-based interactions for managing their own time; dragging a task or meeting across a calendar feels more intuitive and empowering than clicking a dropdown or modal, reducing cognitive load for frequent schedule adjustments
facade.axisTargeted: Motion and Behavior — gesture-driven fluidity vs. form-based rigidity
facade.label: Drag to reschedule
facade.acceptImplies: Freelancers want tactile, continuous interactions that reward physical engagement and feel like direct manipulation of their data rather than submitting requests through UI layers
facade.rejectImplies: Freelancers prefer faster, more intentional interfaces where clicks and modals are efficient enough; gesture interactions might feel slow or imprecise for rapid CRM data entry and task management
